behavior_summary: This script implements a one-shot trigger for a firebomb-like item: when the item is 'U8P_FIREBOMB::hit' (U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit or U8P_FIREBOMB::hit processes), it checks a per-item Q flag and only runs its effect once.
If the per-item Q flag is clear, the script sets Item::setQ(0x01, this) to mark the item used and then performs additional attacker/source checks (if a nonzero param1 was provided).
When a source/attacker parameter is supplied, the code tests whether that reference is an NPC (Item::isNpc) and, if so, whether that NPC is dead (Npc::isDead).
After the guard and optional NPC/dead checks the script invokes METHOD::func0676 which likely executes the firebomb's effect (explosion/damage); the exact effect of func0676 is inferred from context.
notable_control_flow: Entry via process U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit (and an equivalent helper U8P_FIREBOMB::hit): both are processes that respond to U8P_FIREBOMB::hit/U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit events.
Guard: call Item::getQ(this) — if nonzero, the handler returns/does nothing (one-shot behavior).
On first activation: call Item::setQ(0x01, this) to mark the item triggered and prevent future activations.

Optional path: if param1 (attacker/source) != 0 then copy it into temporaries, test Item::isNpc(addressof(local1)); if true then call Npc::isDead(addressof(local2)) and use that result to influence subsequent behavior.
Finally, call METHOD::func0676 to perform the follow-up/effect routine regardless of attacker checks (attacker checks likely only alter preconditions).
open_questions: What exactly does METHOD::func0676 do (explosion, damage application, visual/sound effect)? This is inferred but not present in the excerpt.
What precise semantics does the per-item Q flag represent across the game (is it strictly a one-shot-used marker or also encodes other state)?
Do U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit and U8P_FIREBOMB::hit differ in any engine-facing way (different process types or event origins) beyond identical logic in the excerpt?
How do the NPC dead-check results affect behavior downstream (do they cancel the effect, change target selection, or only record context)?

main_function.name: U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit
main_function.purpose: Handle the item being struck/triggered; ensure the item only triggers once by setting a per-item Q flag, optionally inspect the attacker/source for NPC/death state, then invoke the follow-up effect routine.
entry_conditions: Invoked by the engine when the item receives a 'U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit' event.
Logic proceeds only when Item::getQ(this) returns zero (i.e., not previously triggered).
Optional behavior if caller supplies a nonzero attacker/source param1.

function_map.name: U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit
function_map.role: Primary U8P_FIREBOMB::hit/U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit process that enforces one-shot guard and dispatches the effect.

function_map.name: U8P_FIREBOMB::hit
function_map.role: Helper process that implements effectively the same trigger logic as U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit (different entry/event context inferred).

external_calls.purpose: Read a per-item flag/quantity used as a one-shot guard to determine whether trigger logic should run.
Set the per-item Q flag (0x01) to mark the item as triggered/used, preventing repeated activations.
Test whether a provided source/attacker reference (param1) refers to an NPC before running NPC-specific checks.
If the source is an NPC, test whether that NPC is dead; result likely alters subsequent logic or is used to short-circuit behavior.
Invoke the follow-up/effect routine (inferred to perform the firebomb's explosion/damage/effect).
Same guard read as in U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit (helper mirrors main behavior).
Same per-item flag set as in U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit to mark the item used.
Same source/attacker NPC test as in U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit.
Same NPC dead check as in U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit.
Same follow-up/effect invocation as in U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit.

quick_facts.behavior_summary_one_liner: This script implements a one-shot trigger for a firebomb-like item: when the item is 'U8P_FIREBOMB::hit' (U8P_FIREBOMB::gotHit or U8P_FIREBOMB::hit processes), it checks a per-item Q flag and only runs its effect once.
